figures.gloss: A scholar of Qadian, Punjab, who from 1889 accepted the allegiance of followers first as the divinely appointed renewer (mujaddid) of the age, then as the Promised Messiah and Mahdi awaited by Muslims. He claimed a subordinate, non-law-bearing prophethood entirely within Islam, under Muhammad's seal.
figures.facet: His claim turns on a single Qur'anic phrase: reading 'Seal of the Prophets' as the guarantor of continuing, derivative prophecy rather than its termination — the precise reading Pakistan's 1974 constitutional amendment declared beyond the pale of Islam.
figures.provenance: Barahin-e-Ahmadiyya (1880–84); Ruhani Khaza'in (collected works); Yohanan Friedmann, 'Prophecy Continuous' (1989).

figures.gloss: The elected office of Khalifatul Masih, successor to the Promised Messiah, held continuously since Ghulam Ahmad's death in 1908 — five caliphs to date, the fifth (Mirza Masroor Ahmad) since 2003 — directing a centralized global community of mosques, missions, and presses.
figures.facet: It is a caliphate in exile from the Muslim world that rejects it: Pakistan's 1974 Second Amendment declared Ahmadis non-Muslim and Ordinance XX (1984) criminalized their public Islam, driving the fourth caliph to London that year, where the office has resided since.
figures.provenance: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, Al-Wasiyyat (The Will, 1905); Constitution of Pakistan, Second Amendment (1974); Ordinance XX (1984); Adil Hussain Khan, 'From Sufism to Ahmadiyya' (2015).

figures.gloss: Ghulam Ahmad's ruling that armed jihad stands suspended in this age; the ordained struggle is argument, print, and persuasion. The community answered with translations of the Qur'an into dozens of languages, missionary presses, and the motto 'Love for All, Hatred for None'.
figures.facet: The renunciation has never been reciprocated: the community that disarmed jihad is the one most lethally targeted by takfir — the 1953 Lahore riots and the 2010 attack on two Ahmadi mosques in Lahore that killed more than 80 worshippers.
figures.provenance: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, 'The British Government and Jihad' (1900); motto popularized under the third caliph, Mirza Nasir Ahmad.

figures.gloss: The doctrine that Jesus survived the crucifixion in a swoon, was healed, traveled east in search of the lost tribes of Israel, and died a natural death in Srinagar, Kashmir — where the Roza Bal shrine is held to be his tomb under the name Yuz Asaf.
figures.facet: It engages the dying-and-rising pattern only to dismantle it: a mortal Jesus already dead voids both the Christian resurrection and the mainstream Islamic bodily ascension, clearing the messianic office for Ghulam Ahmad to fill 'in the spirit and power' of Jesus. The Roza Bal identification is rejected by historians and by the shrine's own custodians; it stands here as Ahmadi doctrine, not history.
figures.provenance: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, 'Jesus in India' (Masih Hindustan Mein, written 1899, published 1908).

figures.gloss: Physician-scholar of Bhera (1841-1914), the first person to take the founder's bai'at at Ludhiana in 1889; on Ghulam Ahmad's death in 1908 he was elected the first Khalifatul Masih and held the fledgling community together through six years of consolidation until his own death in 1914.
figures.facet: The office's first occupant sets what it would and would not be — an elected, consensus-built succession rather than a hereditary one, the precedent the four caliphs after him have each followed.
figures.provenance: Adil Hussain Khan, 'From Sufism to Ahmadiyya' (2015); Ahmadiyya community biographical accounts of Nur-ud-Din's caliphate (1908-1914).

figures.gloss: Ghulam Ahmad's son, elected second caliph in 1914 at age 25 over the objections of some of the movement's leading figures; his 51-year tenure (1914-1965) built the community's global missionary structure, including the Tahrik-e-Jadid fund (1934) launched to sustain overseas missions.
figures.facet: His contested election is the fault line of the movement's one great schism — the Lahore group that walked out reads him as a premature, uncredentialed successor, while the community that stayed reads his long tenure as its vindication.
figures.provenance: Adil Hussain Khan, 'From Sufism to Ahmadiyya' (2015); Yohanan Friedmann, 'Prophecy Continuous' (1989).

figures.gloss: Fourth caliph (1982-2003), who left Pakistan under threat after Ordinance XX (1984) criminalized public Ahmadi worship, relocating the khilafat's seat to London — where it has remained since; in 1994 he launched Muslim Television Ahmadiyya, the community's satellite broadcast channel.
figures.facet: He answered a law meant to silence the community by going global and going on air — a caliphate driven underground in Pakistan surfaced instead on satellite television reaching every continent.
figures.provenance: Ordinance XX (1984); Ahmadiyya community accounts of the 1984 relocation to London and the 1994 MTA launch.

figures.gloss: The annual convention instituted by Ghulam Ahmad himself at Qadian in 1891, drawing 75 attendees its first year; now held by national Ahmadi communities worldwide, with the UK's Jalsa Salana — held at the caliph's own seat since the 1980s relocation — the movement's largest single gathering.
figures.facet: A rite that scaled with the community itself: the same founder-instituted convocation now doubles as the visible measure of Ahmadiyya's growth, its attendance cited as evidence of the mission's reach.
figures.provenance: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad's founding announcement (1891); Ahmadiyya community records of Jalsa Salana attendance, Qadian and UK.

figures.gloss: The posthumous compilation, first published in 1935 and enlarged in later editions, of Mirza Ghulam Ahmad's recorded dreams, visions, and verbal revelations drawn from his own diaries and earlier publications; Ahmadis treat it as the register of his revelatory career, distinct from his authored theological works.
figures.facet: Anthology rather than scripture-in-the-moment — the record of what he said God showed him, assembled by the community after his death rather than dictated as a single continuous text.
figures.provenance: Tadhkirah (1st ed. 1935; subsequent enlarged editions); compiled from revelations recorded in Barahin-e-Ahmadiyya and Ruhani Khaza'in.

figures.gloss: Ghulam Ahmad's 1886 Hoshiarpur prayer-retreat prophecy of a promised son of exceptional spiritual and worldly distinction; Mirza Bashir-ud-Din Mahmud Ahmad — born 1889 — publicly declared himself its fulfillment in 1944, an event the community still commemorates annually as Musleh Ma'ud Day.
figures.facet: A prophecy whose fulfillment is claimed by the very figure whose authority it would legitimate — the second caliph reading his own life as the prophecy's referent binds the office's charisma to the founder's original revelatory claim.
figures.provenance: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad's 1886 Hoshiarpur announcement; Mirza Bashir-ud-Din Mahmud Ahmad's 1944 declaration; Friedmann, 'Prophecy Continuous' (1989).

figures.gloss: The 1914 secession led by Muhammad Ali and Khwaja Kamal-ud-Din, who left Qadian on Mahmud Ahmad's election rather than accept his caliphate, forming the Lahore Ahmadiyya Movement (Ahmadiyya Anjuman Isha'at-i-Islam); the split turns on whether Ghulam Ahmad's own claim was to prophethood or only to being a mujaddid (reformer).
figures.facet: The community's one great schism reproduces its founder's central ambiguity — the same 'Seal of the Prophets' question that divides Ahmadis from the wider Muslim world also, in miniature, divides Ahmadis from each other.
figures.provenance: Adil Hussain Khan, 'From Sufism to Ahmadiyya' (2015), a study centered on this split; Friedmann (1989).

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: Ghulam Ahmad claimed only a derivative, non-law-bearing prophethood under Muhammad's seal — the subordination itself is the doctrine, and the reading of that seal is the exact wound Pakistan's anti-Ahmadi laws legislate.
GOLD SEAM: Explicit doctrinal identification of the Roza Bal saint Yuz Asaf with Jesus of Nazareth — attested Ahmadi teaching since 1899, rejected by historians.
GOLD SEAM: Both answer the same wound — how divine guidance continues after the founder — one through an infallible bloodline, the other through an elected caliphate held to be divinely guided.

GOLD SEAM: Contemporaneous 19th-century claimants to the converged Mahdi-and-Messiah office — Bahá'u'lláh exits Islam into a new dispensation, while Ghulam Ahmad insists his office lives entirely inside it.
Intra-tradition: his messianic claim carries the ruling that the sword is retired — the Mahdi's war is fought in print.
Intra-tradition: the khilafat exists solely to steward the Promised Messiah's mission after 1908.
First convert and first elected successor: Nur-ud-Din's caliphate (1908-1914) is the office's founding precedent.
Nur-ud-Din is the Khilafat-i-Ahmadiyya's first holder — the principle instantiated in a person.
Second and longest-reigning caliph, builder of the office's missionary and organizational machinery.
Fourth caliph, who carried the office into exile in London in 1984.
He is the prophecy's self-declared fulfillment, announced in 1944.
His own 1886 Hoshiarpur prophecy, later claimed fulfilled by his son.
His 1914 election is the split's proximate trigger.
The split re-litigates the founder's own prophet/mujaddid ambiguity, dividing his community over the reading of his own claim.
Instituted by the founder himself at Qadian in 1891.
The compiled register of his own recorded revelations.
both institutionalize ongoing prophetic guidance after the founding revelator — an elected caliphate held to be divinely guided against a living-prophet office with an open canon.

Ahmadis explicitly identify Mirza Ghulam Ahmad as the fulfillment of the hadith-predicted Mahdi (and Promised Messiah) — a documented doctrinal claim, not mere resemblance, and precisely the claim that separates Ahmadiyya from mainstream Islam.

Two new religious movements whose founding prophet's death was followed by an enduring line of successor-leadership that carried and consolidated the community — Young's presidency and the Ahmadiyya Khilafat, structurally parallel successions, no historical connection.

dossier.essence: An Islamic messianic movement founded in Qadian, Punjab, where Mirza Ghulam Ahmad accepted the first bai'at (pledge of allegiance) on 23 March 1889, claiming to be the divinely appointed renewer of the age and then the Promised Messiah and Mahdi — a subordinate, non-law-bearing prophet entirely under Muhammad's seal. Adherents, commonly estimated at ten to twenty million worldwide, are concentrated in South Asia, West Africa, and Indonesia, with a large diaspora; Pakistan's 1974 Second Amendment declared them non-Muslim, and Ordinance XX (1984) criminalized their public practice of Islam. The community insists it is nothing other than Islam restored; most of the Muslim world's religious establishments hold the opposite.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmology is Qur'anic: one God, angels, revelation sealed as law with Muhammad, death followed by the intermediate state, resurrection, and judgment. The distinctive claims are hermeneutic — 'Seal of the Prophets' read as guaranteeing continuing, derivative prophecy rather than ending it, and the messianic prophecies fulfilled spiritually rather than literally: Jesus survived the crucifixion, died naturally in Kashmir (the Roza Bal doctrine), and his 'second coming' occurred in the person of Ghulam Ahmad. Armed apocalyptic expectation is correspondingly disarmed — the Mahdi's victory is to be won by argument and print.
dossier.practice: Practice is standard Sunni-derived Islam — the five pillars, mosque worship, Ramadan — organized under a centralized elected caliphate (Khalifatul Masih, continuous since 1908, resident in London since 1984) to which every member is bound by bai'at. The community runs a disciplined system of graded financial contributions (chanda, and the Wasiyyat scheme from Ghulam Ahmad's 1905 will), a global missionary apparatus of presses, Qur'an translations, mosques, and satellite broadcasting, and annual gatherings (Jalsa Salana); the motto is 'Love for All, Hatred for None'. Jihad of the sword is held suspended in this age.
dossier.canon: The Qur'an and hadith remain canonical; alongside them stand Mirza Ghulam Ahmad's roughly ninety books and tracts, collected as the Ruhani Khaza'in — Barahin-e-Ahmadiyya (1880–84), 'Jesus in India' (1908), and Al-Wasiyyat (1905) chief among them.
dossier.reflects: The refusal to let revelation end — a movement that keeps prophecy alive inside a formally closed canon, and pays for that reading with legal excommunication from its own parent faith.
sources: Yohanan Friedmann, 'Prophecy Continuous: Aspects of Ahmadi Religious Thought and Its Medieval Background' (1989)
Adil Hussain Khan, 'From Sufism to Ahmadiyya: A Muslim Minority Movement in South Asia' (2015)
Simon Ross Valentine, 'Islam and the Ahmadiyya Jama'at: History, Belief, Practice' (2008)
